passage.title: Lecture: The Psychology of Motivation
passage.body: Professor: Today I want to talk about something that underlies nearly every decision you make — motivation. Psychologists broadly divide motivation into two categories: intrinsic and extrinsic. Intrinsic motivation comes from within. When you read a book purely because you enjoy it, or solve a puzzle just for the satisfaction of figuring it out, that is intrinsic motivation at work. Extrinsic motivation, on the other hand, is driven by external rewards or pressures — a salary, a grade, or avoiding punishment. Now, early behaviorists believed that external rewards were the primary engine of human behavior. But decades of research have complicated that picture considerably. Studies show that when you introduce an external reward for an activity someone already finds intrinsically enjoyable, their interest in that activity often drops afterward. This is called the overjustification effect. The practical implication is significant: if you want people — students, employees, anyone — to sustain effort over the long term, fostering genuine internal interest is far more powerful than offering prizes.

choices: The tendency to overestimate how much a reward will motivate someone

A feeling of guilt that follows receiving an unearned prize
The process by which external goals become internalized over time
lectureQuestions.explanation: The professor defines the overjustification effect as the drop in intrinsic interest that occurs when an external reward is added to an already enjoyable activity.
wrongReasons: Overestimating reward impact is not what the professor describes.
Guilt is never mentioned in the passage.
Internalization is the opposite of what the overjustification effect describes.

passage.title: Campus Conversation: Adapting to a New Country
passage.body:
Advisor: Come in! What can I help you with today?
Student: Thanks for seeing me. Honestly, I'm struggling more than I expected. I moved here from Brazil three months ago and I thought I'd adapt quickly, but everything feels harder than I imagined.
Advisor: That's very common. What you're describing sounds like culture shock — a normal response to being immersed in an unfamiliar environment.
Student: I feel exhausted all the time and I miss home. Even small things, like how people talk to each other here, feel strange.
Advisor: Culture shock usually goes through stages: a honeymoon phase, then frustration, then gradual adjustment. You're probably in the frustration stage right now.
Student: That actually makes sense. So it does get better?
Advisor: For most people, yes — especially if you stay connected to your home culture while also making an effort to engage locally. Joining a student club or finding a community group can really help.
Student: I hadn't thought about clubs. Maybe that's worth trying.
Advisor: Definitely. And please don't hesitate to use the counseling center if things feel overwhelming.

lectureQuestions.explanation: The advisor explains the stages of culture shock and says the student is 'probably in the frustration stage right now,' based on the exhaustion and sense of strangeness the student describes.
wrongReasons: The honeymoon phase is characterized by excitement, not exhaustion and frustration.
The adjustment phase comes after frustration, which the student has not yet reached.
Integration is the final stage and is not mentioned as the student's current state.

passage.title: Lecture: The Economics of Supply and Demand
passage.body: Professor: At the heart of market economics lies a deceptively simple idea: price is determined by the interaction of supply and demand. When the amount of a good that producers want to sell exactly matches the amount consumers want to buy, the market reaches what economists call equilibrium. At that point, neither buyers nor sellers have reason to change their behavior, so the price stabilizes. But markets are rarely static. A drought might shrink the supply of wheat, pushing prices up. A new technology might suddenly increase supply and push prices down. Consumer tastes shift. Government policies intervene. Each of these forces moves the equilibrium point. Now, the concept of elasticity tells us how sensitive supply or demand is to a change in price. If a small price increase causes a large drop in demand, we say demand is elastic — think luxury goods. If demand barely changes despite a big price shift, it's inelastic — think insulin for diabetics. Understanding elasticity is essential for businesses setting prices, governments designing taxes, and anyone trying to predict how markets will behave.

wrongReasons: Luxury goods are used to illustrate elastic demand, not inelastic.
Wheat is used as an example of a supply shock, not elasticity.
Consumer electronics are mentioned in the context of technology increasing supply, not inelasticity.

passage.title: Office Hours: Dealing With Writer's Block
passage.body:
Student: I've been staring at a blank page for three days. I have no idea how to start my paper and the deadline is next week.
Professor: Writer's block is frustrating, but it's almost universal among writers at every level. What's your topic?
Student: It's about urban green spaces and mental health. I have all the research, but when I sit down to write, nothing comes out.
Professor: That's actually very common — you've done the reading and your brain is overwhelmed with information. Try this: forget about the introduction for now. Write the body first. Start with whichever argument you feel most confident about.
Student: I always thought you had to write in order.
Professor: That's a myth. Writing is iterative — you draft, revise, rearrange. Another trick is timed freewriting: set a timer for ten minutes and write without stopping, even if what comes out is messy. It helps break the psychological barrier.
Student: That sounds more manageable. What about the introduction?
Professor: Write it last. Once you know what you've argued, a strong introduction writes itself.

lectureQuestions.explanation: The professor's first suggestion is to skip the introduction and write the body first, starting with the argument the student feels most confident about.

Writing the introduction first is explicitly discouraged.

passage.title: Lecture: Climate Migration and Its Challenges
passage.body: Professor: Climate change is no longer only an environmental story — it is increasingly a human one. Across the globe, people are being forced to leave their homes not because of war or economic hardship in the traditional sense, but because the land itself is becoming uninhabitable. Rising sea levels are swallowing low-lying islands and coastal communities in countries like Bangladesh and Kiribati. Prolonged droughts are making it impossible to farm in parts of sub-Saharan Africa and Central America. Extreme heat is rendering some urban areas dangerous during summer months. These displaced individuals are often called climate refugees, though that term has no legal recognition under current international law. This is one of the central challenges: existing refugee frameworks were designed for those fleeing persecution, not environmental collapse. Without legal protection, climate migrants fall through the cracks of the international system. Some countries are beginning to experiment with managed retreat — planned relocation of vulnerable communities — but this solution raises its own difficult questions about identity, culture, and consent.

lectureQuestions.text: Why does the professor point out that 'climate refugee' has no legal recognition?
choices: To argue that climate change is exaggerated by the media
To highlight that current international law leaves climate migrants without protection
To suggest that climate migrants do not deserve legal protection
To explain why some countries refuse to accept any type of refugee
lectureQuestions.explanation: The professor explains that existing refugee frameworks cover persecution, not environmental collapse, meaning climate migrants have no legal protection — illustrating a critical gap in international law.
wrongReasons: The professor treats climate migration as a serious reality, not an exaggeration.
The professor expresses concern for climate migrants' lack of protection, not indifference to it.
The lecture does not address countries refusing all refugees.

Rising sea levels are swallowing low-lying islands and coastal communities in countries like Bangladesh and Kiribati. Prolonged droughts are making it impossible to farm in parts of sub-Saharan Africa and Central America. Extreme heat is rendering some urban areas dangerous during summer months. These displaced individuals are often called climate refugees, though that term has no legal recognition under current international law. This is one of the central challenges: existing refugee frameworks were designed for those fleeing persecution, not environmental collapse. Without legal protection, climate migrants fall through the cracks of the international system. Some countries are beginning to experiment with managed retreat — planned relocation of vulnerable communities — but this solution raises its own difficult questions about identity, culture, and consent.

passage.title: Campus Conversation: Choosing Between Two Majors
passage.body:
Student: I really need your advice. I'm supposed to declare my major next month and I'm completely torn between Environmental Science and Computer Science.
Advisor: That's a meaningful choice. What draws you to each one?
Student: I care about climate issues, so Environmental Science feels important. But I'm also really good at coding and there are so many more job opportunities in tech.
Advisor: Those are two different types of pull — passion on one side, practicality on the other. Neither is wrong. Have you considered that these fields can overlap?
Student: What do you mean?
Advisor: There's a growing field called computational sustainability — using data science and algorithms to tackle environmental problems. Some universities even offer dual degrees. But even without that, many environmental organizations now need people with strong technical skills.
Student: I hadn't thought about combining them. I assumed I had to choose one completely.
Advisor: You don't necessarily. Even if you major in one, you can take electives in the other to keep options open. What feels more central to your identity right now?
Student: Honestly, the environmental side. I just worry about job security.
Advisor: That's a valid concern, but the market for environmentally-focused technologists is growing. You might not have to sacrifice as much as you think.

passage.title: Lecture: How Neural Networks Learn
passage.body: Professor: Today I want to demystify one of the most important ideas in modern artificial intelligence: how neural networks actually learn. Imagine a network of interconnected nodes, loosely modeled on the neurons in your brain. Each connection between nodes has a weight — a number that represents how strongly one node influences another. When you feed the network an input, say an image of a cat, the signal travels through those weighted connections and produces an output, like the label 'cat' or 'not cat.' Initially, those weights are random, so the network guesses badly. Here is where learning begins. We compare the network's guess to the correct answer and measure the error. Then we use an algorithm called backpropagation — short for backward propagation of errors — to trace that error back through the network, layer by layer. At each layer, we adjust the weights slightly so the network would have done better on that example. Repeat this process thousands or millions of times across many examples, and the weights gradually tune themselves until the network becomes quite accurate. This is, in essence, how machines learn to recognize faces, translate languages, and predict your next word.

lectureQuestions.explanation: The professor explicitly states that weights are initially random, which is why the network guesses badly at first, and that learning adjusts them over time.
wrongReasons: Engineers do not carefully pre-set the weights; they are random at the start.
Copying weights from another network is not mentioned.

passage.title: Tutorial: Logical Fallacies in Arguments
passage.body:
Tutor: Today we're going to practice spotting logical fallacies — errors in reasoning that make an argument invalid even if it sounds convincing. Let's start with one you've probably encountered: the ad hominem fallacy. Can you tell me what you think it means?
Student: Is it when you attack the person instead of their argument?
Tutor: Exactly right. So if someone says, 'We shouldn't listen to her views on climate policy because she drives an SUV,' that's an ad hominem. Her car habits don't actually address the argument's logic. Now what about the straw man fallacy?
Student: I think that's when you misrepresent what someone said to make it easier to argue against?
Tutor: Perfect. For example: someone says we should reduce sugar in school lunches, and the response is 'So you want to ban all food choices for children.' That's a gross exaggeration of the original claim. The opponent is arguing against a position that was never made. These two fallacies are especially common in political debates. The key question to ask is always: does this response actually address the original claim, or does it deflect from it?

lectureQuestions.text: What example does the tutor give to illustrate the straw man fallacy?
choices: Saying someone should not speak on climate policy because they drive an SUV
Claiming a proposal to reduce school lunch sugar means banning all food choices
Arguing that a politician is dishonest based on their past mistakes
Suggesting that anyone who disagrees with a policy hates children
lectureQuestions.explanation: The tutor gives the example of someone proposing reduced sugar in school lunches, and an opponent responding by claiming the person wants to 'ban all food choices for children' — a gross exaggeration of the original position.
wrongReasons: The SUV example is used to illustrate ad hominem, not straw man.
Calling a politician dishonest based on past mistakes is not discussed in the passage.
Claiming someone 'hates children' is not the example given for any fallacy in the passage.

passage.title: Lecture: The Spread of English as a Global Language
passage.body: Professor: English is spoken today by roughly two billion people, making it the most widely used language in human history. But this didn't happen by accident, and it certainly didn't happen because English is inherently easier or better than other languages. It happened because of power. The first great engine of English's spread was British colonialism. By the nineteenth century, Britain controlled territories across every inhabited continent. In each colony, English was imposed as the language of administration, law, and education. Local languages were often discouraged or outright suppressed. When those colonies gained independence, many chose to retain English — not out of affection for their former rulers, but because it served as a neutral lingua franca among populations speaking dozens of different local languages. The second major driver has been twentieth-century American cultural and economic dominance. Hollywood, the internet, international finance, and the sciences all operate largely in English, creating enormous pressure on individuals worldwide to acquire it. Critics argue this creates linguistic inequality — those born into English-speaking households have a head start unavailable to others. But others point out that a shared global language, however it arose, does facilitate international cooperation.

lectureQuestions.text: Why did many former British colonies retain English after independence, according to the professor?

Because Britain required it as a condition of granting independence
Because English served as a neutral common language among populations with many different local languages
Because English was easier to learn than the native alternatives
lectureQuestions.explanation: The professor states that many post-colonial nations kept English as a lingua franca — a neutral common tongue — not out of loyalty to Britain, but because their own populations spoke dozens of different local languages.
wrongReasons: The professor explicitly says it was not 'out of affection for their former rulers.'
No such British requirement for independence is mentioned.
The professor directly rejects the idea that English is inherently easier than other languages.

passage.title: Campus Conversation: Planning a Research Presentation
passage.body:
Student A: Okay, we have about twenty minutes of presentation time. How do we want to structure the slides?
Student B: I think we should open with our research question — make it really clear what we were trying to find out before we get into method or results.
Student A: Agreed. And then methodology next? Or go straight to results?
Student B: Methodology first — the audience needs to understand how we collected data before the results will mean anything to them.
Student A: Good point. What about the results section — should we use graphs or tables?
Student B: Graphs, definitely. Visual data is easier to read quickly during a presentation. We can put the full tables in the appendix if people want the details.
Student A: That makes sense. And the conclusion — how long?
Student B: Two or three slides max. We want to end clearly: restate the main finding, discuss implications, and suggest future research directions.
Student A: Should we add a Q and A slide at the end?
Student B: Yes, always. It signals to the audience that we welcome questions and it gives us a moment to breathe before they start asking.

lectureQuestions.text: Why does Student B prefer using graphs over tables in the results section?
choices: Graphs contain more data than tables and are therefore more accurate
Tables are not permitted in academic presentations
Graphs are easier for an audience to interpret quickly during a presentation
Their professor specifically requested graphs instead of tables
lectureQuestions.explanation: Student B explicitly says 'Visual data is easier to read quickly during a presentation,' making speed and clarity the reason for preferring graphs over tables.
wrongReasons: The preference is about clarity and speed, not data quantity or accuracy.
No rule against tables in presentations is mentioned; tables are offered as an appendix option.

Student B: I think we should open with our research question — make it really clear what we were trying to find out before we get into method or results.
Student A: Agreed. And then methodology next? Or go straight to results?
Student B: Methodology first — the audience needs to understand how we collected data before the results will mean anything to them.
Student A: Good point. What about the results section — should we use graphs or tables?
Student B: Graphs, definitely. Visual data is easier to read quickly during a presentation. We can put the full tables in the appendix if people want the details.
Student A: That makes sense. And the conclusion — how long?
Student B: Two or three slides max. We want to end clearly: restate the main finding, discuss implications, and suggest future research directions.
Student A: Should we add a Q and A slide at the end?
Student B: Yes, always. It signals to the audience that we welcome questions and it gives us a moment to breathe before they start asking.

lectureQuestions.text: What is Student B's attitude toward the Q&A slide at the end of the presentation?
choices: Opposed, believing it disrupts the flow of the conclusion
Neutral, saying it is optional depending on the audience
Strongly in favor, viewing it as both a signal to the audience and a personal benefit
Uncertain, wanting to check with the professor before deciding
lectureQuestions.explanation: Student B says 'Yes, always,' indicating strong support, and gives two reasons: it signals openness to questions and gives the presenters a moment to collect themselves.
wrongReasons: Student B is clearly in favor of the Q&A slide, not opposed to it.
Student B says 'always,' not 'optional.' The stance is definitive.
No mention of consulting the professor is made.

lectureQuestions.text: What is the central argument of this lecture?
choices: Getting more than eight hours of sleep leads to emotional numbness
Sleep deprivation impairs the brain's ability to regulate emotional responses
Dreaming is the most important function of sleep for emotional health
Emotional problems are the primary cause of sleep disorders
lectureQuestions.explanation: The professor's central argument is that inadequate sleep disrupts emotional regulation, specifically through its effects on the amygdala and prefrontal cortex.
wrongReasons: The professor discusses insufficient sleep, not excessive sleep.
Dreaming is not mentioned; the focus is on sleep quantity and brain function.
The lecture describes sleep deprivation causing emotional problems, not the reverse.

passage.title: Lecture: The Role of Sleep in Emotional Regulation
passage.body: Professor: We tend to think of sleep as passive — a period of inactivity when the body simply rests. But neuroscience has revealed that the sleeping brain is extraordinarily active, and one of its most critical jobs is processing and regulating emotion. At the center of this is a small almond-shaped structure called the amygdala, which is responsible for detecting threats and triggering fear and anger responses. When we are well-rested, the prefrontal cortex — the rational, decision-making part of the brain — keeps the amygdala in check. But after even one night of poor sleep, studies show the amygdala becomes up to sixty percent more reactive to negative stimuli. The prefrontal cortex essentially goes offline, losing its ability to moderate the emotional alarm system. This is why sleep-deprived people are more irritable, more anxious, and more prone to emotional outbursts. The implications extend beyond bad moods. Chronic sleep deprivation has been linked to anxiety disorders, depression, and impaired empathy. If you're struggling emotionally and also sleeping poorly, the relationship between those two facts is probably not coincidental.

lectureQuestions.text: According to the professor, what happens to the amygdala after one night of poor sleep?
choices: It shrinks in size, reducing emotional responses
It becomes up to sixty percent more reactive to negative stimuli
It temporarily shuts down, causing emotional numbness
It begins producing excess cortisol, leading to chronic stress
lectureQuestions.explanation: The professor states that studies show the amygdala becomes 'up to sixty percent more reactive to negative stimuli' after even one night of poor sleep.
wrongReasons: The amygdala becomes more reactive, not smaller or less reactive.
The amygdala does not shut down; rather it becomes hyperreactive when the prefrontal cortex goes offline.
Cortisol production is not mentioned in the lecture.

To discuss a failing grade and find out how to improve
To appeal a grade they believe was marked incorrectly

passage.title: Office Hours: Discussing a Failing Grade
passage.body:
Student: I got a fifty-two on the midterm and I'm really worried. I studied for hours but I don't know what went wrong.
Professor: I'm glad you came in. A lot of students don't, and that's a missed opportunity. Let me pull up your exam. Looking here, I can see the main problem isn't your knowledge of the content — your short answers are actually pretty solid. The issue is with the essay question. You listed a lot of relevant facts, but you didn't construct an argument.
Student: What do you mean by that?
Professor: In an academic essay, facts alone aren't enough. You need a clear thesis — a central claim — and then your evidence has to build toward proving that claim. Your essay read more like a list than an argument.
Student: I think I see. How do I fix that?
Professor: Before you write a single sentence, write your thesis down. Then ask yourself: does each paragraph support this specific claim? If a paragraph doesn't connect to your thesis, cut it or rework it. Come see me with a draft outline before the final exam and I'll give you feedback.
Student: That would really help. Thank you.
Professor: Of course. One bad exam doesn't define the course. There's still plenty of opportunity.

lectureQuestions.text: According to the professor, what was the specific weakness in the student's exam essay?
choices: It contained factual errors about the course content
It was too short to adequately address the question
It listed facts without constructing a central argument or thesis
It was written in an informal style inappropriate for academic work
lectureQuestions.explanation: The professor identifies the problem as structural: the student listed relevant facts but did not construct an argument built around a clear thesis — the essay 'read more like a list than an argument.'
wrongReasons: The professor says the short answers showed solid content knowledge; the facts in the essay were described as relevant, not wrong.
Essay length is not discussed as a problem.
Writing style or formality is not mentioned as an issue.
